turns.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="512" height="512" viewBox="0 0 512 512">
  <defs>
    <radialGradient id="gGlow" cx="50%" cy="65%" r="50%">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffd77a" stop-opacity="0.9"/>
      <stop offset="60%" stop-color="#ff8a3a" stop-opacity="0.25"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#ff6a2b" stop-opacity="0"/>
    </radialGradient>
    <linearGradient id="gOuter" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffb84d"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#ff6a00"/>
    </linearGradient>
    <linearGradient id="gMid" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffd36b"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#ff3b00"/>
    </linearGradient>
    <linearGradient id="gInner" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffffff"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#ff9a00"/>
    </linearGradient>
    <linearGradient id="gLog" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="0">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#6b3b18"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#3f2513"/>
    </linearGradient>
  </defs>

  <!-- background -->
  <rect width="100%" height="100%" fill="#0b0b0b" />

  <!-- soft glow behind the flame -->
  <ellipse cx="256" cy="320" rx="160" ry="120" fill="url(#gGlow)" opacity="0.9" />

  <!-- logs -->
  <g transform="translate(256,420)">
    <rect x="-140" y="-18" width="280" height="36" rx="18" fill="url(#gLog)" transform="rotate(-20)" />
    <rect x="-140" y="-18" width="280" height="36" rx="18" fill="url(#gLog)" transform="rotate(20)" />
  </g>

  <!-- outer flame -->
  <path d="M256 420 C170 420 120 320 170 250 C200 210 240 160 256 110 C272 160 312 210 342 250 C392 320 332 420 256 420 Z"
        fill="url(#gOuter)"/>

  <!-- middle flame -->
  <path d="M256 380 C200 380 160 320 200 260 C230 220 256 170 256 140 C256 170 292 220 312 260 C352 320 312 380 256 380 Z"
        fill="url(#gMid)"/>

  <!-- inner core -->
  <path d="M256 320 C232 320 200 280 224 240 C236 220 256 180 256 150 C256 180 278 220 288 240 C312 280 288 320 256 320 Z"
        fill="url(#gInner)"/>

  <!-- small sparks -->
  <g fill="#ffd76a" opacity="0.9">
    <circle cx="190" cy="200" r="6" />
    <circle cx="330" cy="190" r="5" />
    <circle cx="260" cy="90" r="7" />
    <circle cx="220" cy="260" r="4" />
    <circle cx="300" cy="250" r="4" />
  </g>
</svg>

messages.content:
You are a drawing agent in a one-word guessing game.
Return JSON only, with a single field: svg.

Rules:
1) Draw the target word using only SVG graphics.
2) Output one complete <svg>...</svg> string.
3) Do not include text labels, letters, or numbers in the drawing.
4) Keep the SVG simple and valid. No scripts, no foreignObject.
5) Use a 512x512 canvas.

Output schema:
{ "svg": "string" }

message.content:
{
  "svg": "<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="512" height="512" viewBox="0 0 512 512">
  <defs>
    <radialGradient id="gGlow" cx="50%" cy="65%" r="50%">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffd77a" stop-opacity="0.9"/>
      <stop offset="60%" stop-color="#ff8a3a" stop-opacity="0.25"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#ff6a2b" stop-opacity="0"/>
    </radialGradient>
    <linearGradient id="gOuter" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffb84d"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#ff6a00"/>
    </linearGradient>
    <linearGradient id="gMid" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffd36b"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#ff3b00"/>
    </linearGradient>
    <linearGradient id="gInner" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffffff"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#ff9a00"/>
    </linearGradient>
    <linearGradient id="gLog" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="0">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#6b3b18"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#3f2513"/>
    </linearGradient>
  </defs>

  <!-- background -->
  <rect width="100%" height="100%" fill="#0b0b0b" />

  <!-- soft glow behind the flame -->
  <ellipse cx="256" cy="320" rx="160" ry="120" fill="url(#gGlow)" opacity="0.9" />

  <!-- logs -->
  <g transform="translate(256,420)">
    <rect x="-140" y="-18" width="280" height="36" rx="18" fill="url(#gLog)" transform="rotate(-20)" />
    <rect x="-140" y="-18" width="280" height="36" rx="18" fill="url(#gLog)" transform="rotate(20)" />
  </g>

  <!-- outer flame -->
  <path d="M256 420 C170 420 120 320 170 250 C200 210 240 160 256 110 C272 160 312 210 342 250 C392 320 332 420 256 420 Z"
        fill="url(#gOuter)"/>

  <!-- middle flame -->
  <path d="M256 380 C200 380 160 320 200 260 C230 220 256 170 256 140 C256 170 292 220 312 260 C352 320 312 380 256 380 Z"
        fill="url(#gMid)"/>

  <!-- inner core -->
  <path d="M256 320 C232 320 200 280 224 240 C236 220 256 180 256 150 C256 180 278 220 288 240 C312 280 288 320 256 320 Z"
        fill="url(#gInner)"/>

  <!-- small sparks -->
  <g fill="#ffd76a" opacity="0.9">
    <circle cx="190" cy="200" r="6" />
    <circle cx="330" cy="190" r="5" />
    <circle cx="260" cy="90" r="7" />
    <circle cx="220" cy="260" r="4" />
    <circle cx="300" cy="250" r="4" />
  </g>
</svg>"
}
message.reasoning:
**Designing fire SVG**

I'm considering adding a subtle glow using a radial gradient, and I think about how to incorporate logs as brown rounded rectangles. However, using logs might suggest a "campfire" instead of just "fire." It might be better to avoid them for ambiguity, focusing on the flames.

To compose the SVG, I’ll definitely include various gradients for color and a dark background to create contrast. A black background could simplify the design. Let's put it all together!**Crafting flame shapes**

I'm working on drawing a radial glow with the gGlow centered near the bottom, and then I’ll create the outer flame path shape. I want to use path commands to define flame shapes approximately centered at (256, 320). To achieve three flame shapes, I'll use Bézier curves. 

While crafting the paths, I’m realizing that symmetry would enhance the design. I can mirror curves for a more controlled shape, although precise details aren't critical. Let's make it visually appealing!**Designing flame layers and logs**

I'm laying out the mid and inner flame shapes, using Bézier curves for both designs. They might not be perfect, but they work. I’m also considering adding small spark elements as circles filled with a soft yellow and orange, plus some opacity for effect. 

For the ground embers, I’ll include a couple of small logs as rounded rectangles that intersect at the bottom. I’ll use the rx property for rounded corners and apply a rotation. I just need to ensure the JSON string is properly formatted!**Creating SVG JSON**

I'm looking to create a one-line string for the JSON, making sure to use double quotes around the values and escaping any double quotes within. I’ll also include newline characters where needed. It’s important to check for any banned elements like scripts or text to keep things clean. 

Once I’ve confirmed everything is valid XML with properly closed tags, I’ll assemble the final SVG string and produce the JSON. Let’s make sure it’s all set!

messages.content:
You are the guesser in a visual one-word guessing game.
Return JSON only, with a single field: guesses.

Rules:
1) You receive one JPEG image of a drawing.
2) Return exactly 20 one-word guesses in order of confidence (best guess first).
3) The order is important and will be scored by first correct position.
4) Do not repeat guesses.
5) Each guess must be EN-US one word, no spaces.
6) Avoid punctuation and numbers.

Output schema:
{ "guesses": ["string", "... exactly 20 items ..."] }

message.reasoning:
**Generating guesses for campfire icon**

I need to output a JSON array with 20 one-word guesses related to an image of a campfire icon. The shapes look like "campfire," "fire," "flame," and a few others like "ember," "torch," and "candle." I should avoid repeats, keep it single-word, and order them by confidence. For instance, "campfire" is likely my top guess, followed by others like "bonfire," "heat," and "burning." I’ll organize that list!**Crafting unique guesses**

I need to ensure I use common one-word guesses without hyphens or punctuation. I’m aiming for 20 unique options. Some good choices include "campfire," "fire," "flame," "bonfire," and "blaze," progressing through options like "ember," "torch," "candle," "spark," and "inferno." I should check if "smolder," "kindle," and "pyro" work since they're one word but might be considered ambiguous. I'm also double-checking for duplicates. Once I have my list settled, I can format it as JSON!**Finalizing guesses by confidence**

I'm finalizing my list of guesses ordered by confidence. I’ll start with "campfire" at the top, followed by "fire," "flame," "bonfire," and so on, including "ember," "fireplace," and "hearth." I should make sure to include "tinder" and "logs," but avoid duplicates, especially with "ember" and "embers." For the final entry, I'm considering "ash" or "embers" while maintaining uniqueness. Once I have 20 distinct words, I can format everything into JSON and be done!
